title: Best Monitor Stand 2026: 5 options compared
description: From Uplift Desk Monitor Arm V2 (gas-spring, 7–53cm height range, 360° rotation, VESA 75/100mm, holds 2–9kg) to Ergotron LX (gas-spring, 7–46cm height range, 15kg max load, full-motion tilt/pan/rotate, VESA 75/100mm) to Vari Single Monitor Arm (gas-spring, adjustable 15–50cm, VESA 75/100mm, cable management channel) to Sanwa Supply CR-LA1201 (Japan-market fixed-height desk riser, 13cm lift, 24kg load capacity, cable tray, steel construction) and FlexiSpot F7L (gas-spring dual-arm, 16–53cm, VESA 75/100mm, 9kg per arm). Five stands compared on height/tilt/rotation range, cable management, desk clamp stability, and monitor size compatibility.
lede: Five monitor stands — from the Ergotron LX that has set the standard for gas-spring arms for fifteen years to the Sanwa Supply CR-LA1201 that holds a 24kg display and a full keyboard tray. Monitor position is permanent enough that getting it wrong costs you a year of neck strain before you notice the pattern.
methodology: Each product was evaluated against documented specifications, third-party benchmarks, and verified user reports. We scored height range, load capacity, cable management quality, desk clamp stability, and long-term reliability from 12+ month user reports.

recommendedFor.label: For single monitors up to 9kg with maximum adjustability
recommendedFor.reason: Ergotron LX's gas-spring mechanism holds position reliably across its full 7–46cm height range with one-handed repositioning.

recommendedFor.label: For dual-monitor setups where one arm handles each screen
recommendedFor.reason: FlexiSpot F7L's dual gas-spring design manages two monitors independently without a center-post collision risk.

recommendedFor.label: For Japan-based buyers needing a heavy-load fixed riser
recommendedFor.reason: Sanwa CR-LA1201 handles up to 24kg, integrates a cable tray, and is available at major Japanese electronics retailers.

recommendedFor.label: For standing desk users who reposition their monitor frequently
recommendedFor.reason: Uplift V2 arm's 7–53cm range covers both seated and standing desk heights with a single arm.

recommendedFor.label: For clean desk aesthetics with integrated cable routing
recommendedFor.reason: Vari's single arm routes cables through the arm channel for a clean desk surface without exposed wiring.

sections.heading: How we compared
paragraphs: We did not test each arm's gas-spring tension under varying monitor weights with a force gauge. We did not measure desk clamp bite force or surface deformation on different desk materials. Rigorous monitor arm testing requires a calibrated force gauge to measure gas-spring preload accuracy across the specified weight range, and a desk surface deformation measurement to quantify clamp damage risk on different materials — neither of which was available for independent testing here.

Instead: we reviewed manufacturer specifications for height range, VESA pattern compatibility, weight capacity, and desk clamp maximum thickness. We cross-referenced with verified long-term user reviews from Amazon JP, Rakuten Ichiba, Wirecutter, and RTings, specifically seeking reports of gas-spring sag after 12+ months, desk clamp marking or damage on different surface materials, and cable routing quality in practice. We also evaluated retail availability in Japan, since Sanwa Supply and Elecom products are stocked at major electronics chains while Ergotron and Uplift require online import or specialist retailers.

A calibration note on monitor arms: the gas-spring tension on all five products is factory-set for a midpoint weight in the specified range. Users who mount a monitor at the light end of the range (e.g., a 2kg panel on an arm rated for 2–9kg) may find the arm wants to rise; users at the heavy end may find it wants to fall. Most arms in this comparison include a tension adjustment mechanism — a hex key set-screw on the spring unit — that lets you fine-tune for your specific monitor weight. The Ergotron LX and Uplift V2 are both known for having accessible, reliable tension adjustment.

sections.heading: What changed in 2026
paragraphs: Gas-spring quality has converged at the ¥10,000–¥20,000 tier. In 2023, meaningful differences existed between Ergotron's precision spring mechanism and budget competitors that used weaker springs with early sag. By 2026, brands including Uplift and Vari have matched Ergotron's spring quality at equivalent price points, and the differentiation has shifted to cable management quality, desk clamp design, and product warranty terms.

Ultrawide monitor compatibility has become a real spec to check. As 34-inch and 38-inch ultrawide monitors have proliferated — with weights frequently exceeding 9kg and widths that change the arm's effective center of gravity — arms must now explicitly rate their compatibility. The Ergotron LX and Uplift V2 both rate to 9kg, which covers most 34-inch ultrawides but is marginal for 38-inch or 49-inch super-ultrawides. The FlexiSpot F7L rates to 9kg per arm, making it viable for two 34-inch monitors.

Japanese-market fixed risers from Sanwa Supply have added cable trays as standard. Earlier Sanwa riser models offered a platform and nothing else. The CR-LA1201 and newer models include a steel cable tray beneath the platform for routing power bricks and hub cables — a genuinely useful addition that Japanese buyers had been requesting in reviews for several years.

sections.heading: Where each fits
paragraphs: Single monitor up to 9kg, 7–46cm height range, full-motion tilt ±90°, pan 180°, VESA 75/100mm, the gold-standard gas-spring: Ergotron LX. The LX has fifteen years of reliability track record across professional and consumer contexts. Its gas-spring tension adjustment is accessible via hex key without disassembly, the desk clamp holds materials up to 6.4cm thick, and the cable management channel accommodates two medium cables. Available at Yodobashi Camera and Amazon JP. The honest tradeoff: the LX's 9kg maximum load is marginal for 32-inch+ monitors, which can weigh 8–10kg depending on stand base; at ¥15,000–¥20,000 it is mid-tier priced; and the arm extension reach (up to 49cm from post) may be insufficient for desks where the monitor needs to be farther from the rear edge.

Standing desk use, 7–53cm height range wider than the Ergotron LX, VESA 75/100mm, 2–9kg load: Uplift Desk Monitor Arm V2. The 53cm top-end height of the V2 is the widest range in this comparison — relevant for standing desk users who need the arm to rise high enough at standing height without the monitor hitting its stop point. At a similar price to the Ergotron LX (¥18,000–¥25,000), the V2's advantage is the extended range rather than superior spring mechanism. The honest tradeoff: Uplift Desk is a US brand that requires importing to Japan — it is not available at Japanese electronics retail chains, and warranty service is more complicated than domestic brands.

Dual-monitor setup, two independent gas-spring arms on one desk clamp, 16–53cm range, 9kg per arm, VESA 75/100mm: FlexiSpot F7L. Two independent arms for two monitors is a fundamentally better setup than a single dual-monitor post because each arm can be positioned independently — one at eye level, one slightly offset — without the mechanical conflict you get on center-post dual setups where both arms fight for the same rotation plane. At ¥25,000–¥35,000 it costs more than two individual single arms in most cases, but saves a desk clamp slot. The honest tradeoff: dual-arm setups require a desk edge with no drawer, cable tray, or other obstruction along approximately 20cm of usable clamping surface.

Japan-market, fixed height 13cm lift, 24kg load capacity, integrated cable tray, steel construction: Sanwa Supply CR-LA1201. The CR-LA1201 is not a monitor arm — it is a raised platform that sits on the desk and holds the monitor. It does not articulate, tilt, or pan independently of the monitor's own stand mechanism. What it does: raises a monitor (or anything, including a docking station) by 13cm, handles up to 24kg which covers any consumer monitor plus accessories, and includes a cable tray underneath for routing. Available at Yodobashi Camera, Bic Camera, and Amazon JP. The honest tradeoff: it is fixed height and takes up significant desk footprint — the platform dimensions are 60cm wide, which means it occupies desk space that might otherwise be used for keyboard or notebooks.

Single monitor, cable routing through arm channel, 15–50cm range, clean desk aesthetics priority: Vari Single Monitor Arm. Vari's arm routes cables through a channel in the arm body — not cable clips or velcro loops, but an actual enclosed channel — which produces a cleaner cable run from monitor to desk surface than most alternatives. At approximately ¥20,000–¥28,000 it is in the same tier as the Ergotron LX and Uplift V2, and its differentiation is primarily the cable management execution and build quality. The honest tradeoff: Vari is a US brand requiring import to Japan, and the arm reach is shorter than the Uplift V2's extended range.

sections.heading: Verdict
paragraphs: For a single monitor up to 9kg where you want a proven, adjustable gas-spring arm with reliable tension adjustment and Japanese retail availability: Ergotron LX. Buy it from Yodobashi Camera or Amazon JP. Spend ten minutes calibrating the spring tension to your specific monitor weight on installation.

For standing desk use where the monitor needs to reach higher than the Ergotron LX's 46cm ceiling: Uplift Desk V2. Accept that you are importing a US product. The 53cm range ceiling is worth it if you work at standing height for significant portions of the day.

For dual monitors where you want two independent arms on one desk: FlexiSpot F7L. The ¥25,000–¥35,000 price is real, but managing two monitors on independent arms is qualitatively better than center-post setups.

For Japan retail availability, heavy monitors, and a fixed-height riser with cable tray: Sanwa Supply CR-LA1201. Accept the fixed height — it is not a dynamic arm. Accept the desk footprint. For users who want their monitor raised and cable-managed without the complexity of a gas-spring arm, it is the correct answer.

For cable management as the primary priority with a single arm: Vari Single Monitor Arm. The enclosed cable channel is the best cable routing execution in this comparison. Import from the US and accept the same warranty tradeoffs as the Uplift V2.

products.review: The Uplift Desk Monitor Arm V2 extends the height range ceiling to 53cm — the widest in this comparison — which matters for standing desk users who need their monitor to track upward as the desk rises. Gas-spring mechanism, VESA 75/100mm compatibility, 2–9kg load range, and desk clamp accommodating up to 6.8cm thickness. At ¥18,000–¥25,000 it competes directly with the Ergotron LX on price and matches it on spring quality. The honest weaknesses: Uplift Desk is a US brand requiring import to Japan — not stocked at domestic electronics chains, and warranty service requires working with the US-based customer support process.
pros: 7–53cm height range — widest in comparison, covers sitting and standing desk positions
Gas-spring with accessible hex-key tension adjustment for 2–9kg monitors
VESA 75/100mm compatible with desk clamp for material up to 6.8cm thick
Cable management channel runs along arm length
cons: US import required — not available at Japanese electronics retail chains
9kg maximum load is marginal for large 32-inch+ monitors above 8kg

products.review: The Ergotron LX has been the gas-spring monitor arm standard for fifteen years because it does the core function correctly without failure. Height range 7–46cm, tilt ±90°, pan 180°, rotation 360°, VESA 75/100mm, 2–9kg load range. The gas-spring tension is adjusted via an accessible hex set-screw that does not require disassembly. At ¥15,000–¥20,000 it is the most widely available quality arm in Japan — stocked at Yodobashi Camera and with reliable Amazon JP fulfillment. The honest weaknesses: 9kg maximum load is marginal for 32-inch+ monitors near 10kg; 46cm height ceiling is lower than the Uplift V2's 53cm, which matters for standing desk users at full height; arm reach caps at 49cm from post.
pros: Fifteen years of reliability data in professional and consumer contexts
7–46cm height range with ±90° tilt, 180° pan, 360° rotation
Gas-spring tension accessible via hex key without disassembly
Available at Yodobashi Camera and Amazon JP with reliable domestic fulfillment
cons: 46cm height ceiling falls short for full standing desk height on tall desks
9kg maximum load marginal for 32-inch+ monitors approaching 10kg

products.review: The Vari Single Monitor Arm routes cables through an enclosed channel in the arm body — not cable clips or velcro loops — which is the cleanest cable management execution of any arm in this comparison. Height range 15–50cm, VESA 75/100mm, load up to 9kg. At ¥20,000–¥28,000 it competes with the Ergotron LX and Uplift V2 on price, with cable routing as the primary differentiation. The honest weaknesses: Vari is a US brand requiring import to Japan — not available at domestic electronics chains; the 15cm lower height limit is higher than the Ergotron LX's 7cm (a floor-height difference that some desk setups notice); reach is shorter than the Uplift V2.
pros: Enclosed cable channel through arm body — cleanest cable routing in comparison
15–50cm height range with VESA 75/100mm and up to 9kg load
Solid build quality with reliable gas-spring tension
Clean desk aesthetic with no exposed wiring
cons: US import required — not stocked at Japanese electronics retail
15cm lower height limit is higher than Ergotron LX's 7cm floor

products.review: The Sanwa Supply CR-LA1201 is a fixed-height steel riser platform, not a gas-spring arm. It raises a monitor by 13cm, holds up to 24kg, and includes a cable tray beneath the platform. Available at Yodobashi Camera, Bic Camera, and Amazon JP with next-day delivery. The 24kg load capacity covers any consumer monitor plus peripheral accessories. For users who want their monitor raised and cable-managed without the complexity or cost of a gas-spring arm, this is the most direct answer. The honest weakness: it is fixed at 13cm — there is no height adjustment, tilt adjustment, or pan — and the 60cm platform width takes up desk space that is no longer available for keyboard or notebooks.
pros: 24kg load capacity — handles any consumer monitor including large 32-inch+ panels
Integrated cable tray beneath platform for power and hub cable routing
Available same-day at Yodobashi Camera, Bic Camera, and Amazon JP
Steel construction — stable with no wobble under heavy monitors
cons: Fixed 13cm height — no adjustment, tilt, or pan capability
60cm wide platform occupies significant desk surface area

products.review: The FlexiSpot F7L provides two independent gas-spring arms on one desk clamp, each rated for up to 9kg, covering 16–53cm height range per arm. Two independent arms are qualitatively better than a center-post dual setup because each arm can be positioned without mechanical conflict. At ¥25,000–¥35,000 it costs more than two single arms from most brands, but saves a desk clamp slot. Compatible with VESA 75/100mm. The honest weaknesses: dual-arm setups require an unobstructed desk edge of approximately 20cm for the clamp; the 16cm lower height limit is higher than single-arm alternatives; and the combined weight of two monitors plus two arms at full extension creates more torque on the desk clamp than any single-arm setup.
pros: Two independent gas-spring arms for two monitors — avoids center-post mechanical conflicts
9kg per arm — compatible with most 27–32 inch monitors
16–53cm height range per arm covers sitting and most standing desk positions
Single desk clamp footprint for a two-monitor setup
cons: 16cm lower height limit higher than single-arm alternatives at 7cm
Requires approximately 20cm of unobstructed desk edge for clamp

faqs.q: What VESA pattern do I need to check before buying a monitor arm?
faqs.a: VESA mounting patterns are defined by the distance in millimeters between the four mounting holes on the back of the monitor. The two most common patterns in consumer monitors are 75×75mm and 100×100mm. All five arms in this comparison support both. Some larger monitors — particularly 32-inch and above — use 200×100mm or 200×200mm patterns. Check your monitor's manual or the manufacturer's specifications page for 'VESA pattern' or 'VESA mount' before buying any arm. If your monitor does not have VESA holes at all (some budget monitors and ultraslims omit them), you need an adapter bracket — these exist for most major monitor models but add cost and reduce aesthetics.

faqs.q: How heavy is my monitor, and does it matter for gas-spring arms?
faqs.a: Monitor weight matters significantly for gas-spring arms. The gas-spring mechanism is factory-set for a weight range, and a monitor at the edges of that range — either much lighter or much heavier than the midpoint — will not stay in position without tension adjustment. A 27-inch 1440p monitor typically weighs 5–7kg without the stand base. A 32-inch 4K monitor typically weighs 7–10kg. A 34-inch ultrawide typically weighs 7–10kg. A 38-inch ultrawide or 49-inch super-ultrawide can exceed 10kg. For monitors at 9kg or above, check the arm's maximum rated load explicitly — the Ergotron LX's 9kg limit is frequently cited, but real-world 9kg monitors may put it near the edge of reliable holding. The Sanwa CR-LA1201 fixed riser at 24kg load handles any monitor without concern.

faqs.q: Will a monitor arm damage my desk surface?
faqs.a: The desk clamp on all five products in this comparison uses a pad between the clamp jaw and desk surface to prevent direct metal-to-desk contact. However, clamping force from a gas-spring arm under the weight of a heavy monitor can leave impressions in softer desk surfaces — particularly IKEA LINNMON particleboard tops, thin MDF, and hollow-core doors used as budget desks. The solutions: use a wide clamping pad (some arms include an extended pad option), add a folded piece of thin plywood or dense foam between clamp and desk surface, or use a grommet mount through a desk hole rather than a clamp (some desks have pre-drilled grommet holes). The Sanwa CR-LA1201 riser sits on the desk surface without clamping and does not create this issue.

pinDescription: 5 monitor stands compared for 2026 — Ergotron LX gold standard vs Uplift V2 for standing desks vs Vari cable management vs Sanwa Supply 24kg fixed riser for Japan vs FlexiSpot F7L dual-arm. VESA compatibility guide included. Desk setup starts here.
